一位英国艺术历史学家对圣地亚哥·德·孔波斯特拉的大教堂进行了艰苦的研究，发现了一个中世纪的恶作剧。
他是中世纪的笑柄，12世纪初为世界上最伟大的大教堂之一雕刻的男性形象，但直到现在还没有人知道他的存在。几个世纪以来，数百万朝圣者长途跋涉前往西班牙西北部加利西亚的圣地亚哥·德·孔波斯特拉(Santiago De Compostela)朝圣，但这个数字并没有引起数百万朝拜者的注意。他从高高耸立的众多柱子中的一根柱子的顶部俯视着它们，每根柱子上都装饰着雕刻的树叶，他就藏在其中。
现在，一位英国艺术学者发现了他，这位学者认为，他实际上从来就不是要被人看到的，因为他是一位12世纪在大教堂工作的石匠的自画像。
詹妮弗·亚历山大博士告诉“观察家报”：“你可以在中世纪的建筑中发现这一点。”“它们通常在黑暗的角落里，只有另一个石匠才能找到它们。这个是在大楼的一小块地方，你必须是一个石匠才能在那里看到它。这是一座建筑的一小部分，你必须是一个石匠才能在那里看到它。它被藏在一整组大写字母(柱子的顶部)之间，否则它们就很普通了。
“这是我们和雕刻者之间如此迷人的联系。它几乎就像是专门设计给我们看的，是那些在大楼里工作的人设计的。当然，这位石匠可能不知道他要等这么久才能被发现。“。
尽管这些工匠才华横溢，但他们完全默默无闻，他们的名字消失在历史中。这是泥瓦匠离签名最近的一次了。
亚历山大是华威大学艺术史的读书人，是研究中世纪大教堂和大教堂建筑史的专家。
她在对联合国教科文组织世界遗产圣地亚哥·德·孔波斯特拉大教堂进行复杂的调查时发现了这个数字。
它的意义在于它与卡米诺的联系，卡米诺是穿越数百英里到达大教堂内的使徒圣詹姆斯神殿的朝圣之旅。这就是它的吸引力所在。仅在2019年，到达该市的官方朝圣者人数就约为35万人。
目前的建筑始建于11世纪末，是罗马式建筑的杰作，后来又增加了一些。
亚历山大正在进行一块石头的分析，以确定其建设顺序，这是由加利西亚地区政府资助的一个项目。她回忆说，正是当她研究人行道上方约13米处的首都时，“这个小身影突然冒了出来”。
“一个可爱的小伙子紧抓着首都的中心，仿佛他的生命依赖于此。它位于一排完全相同的现成首都中，他们一直在用花岗岩将它们击倒-“我们还需要15个那种设计”-突然之间就有了一个不同的。所以我们认为是这个人自己干的。
“他走出首都，紧紧抓住它不放。这几乎就像是在吞噬他。“。
这个高约30厘米的雕像被描绘到他的腰部。亚历山大说：“他有一个可爱的小微笑。他对自己很满意。他雕刻精美，有一张很有个性的脸。“。
中世纪的石匠通过学徒的方式学会了他们的技艺，这些学徒训练他们从事切割石头或使用模板在门口和其他开口周围制作复杂的模子的艰苦行业。最有前途的泥瓦匠会学习几何，这样他们就可以设计平面图和管理建筑工地。
亚历山大说：“这些大师必须有很多技能，因为他们还负责工程、材料供应、雇佣劳动力和与赞助人打交道，赞助人可能是高级神职人员或贵族。”
她补充说，这样的工匠在几个世纪里一直保持匿名，甚至到最近也是如此：“当他们在20世纪建造利物浦大教堂时，他们公布了在这座建筑上工作的工匠的名单，他们从来没有提到过石匠。所以这些就是无名之辈了。“
note_en:
A British art historian’s painstaking study of the cathedral in Santiago de Compostela uncovered a medieval prank
    H e is a medieval in-joke, a male figure carved in the early 12th century for one of the world’s greatest cathedrals, but no one has known of his existence until now. The figure has gone unnoticed by millions of worshippers who have made the long pilgrimage to  Santiago de Compostela in Galicia, north-western Spain over the centuries. He has looked down on them from the top of one of the many pillars that soar upwards, each decorated with carved foliage, among which he is concealed.
 Now he has been discovered by a British art scholar who believes that he was actually never meant to be seen because he is a self-portrait of a stonemason who worked on the cathedral in the 12th century.
  “You find this in medieval buildings,” Dr Jennifer Alexander told the  Observer. “They’re usually in dark corners where only another stonemason would find them. This one is in a bit of the building where you’d have to be a stonemason to be up there to see it. It’s tucked away in among a whole set of capitals [the top of a column] that are otherwise plain.
  “It’s just such a charming connection between us and the person that carved it. It’s almost as if it was designed just for us to see it by those people working on the building. Of course, this stonemason probably had no idea that he’d have to wait so long to be spotted.”
 Despite the supreme talent of such craftsmen, they were completely anonymous, their names lost to history. This is the closest the mason got to signing his work.
 Alexander, a reader in art history at the University of Warwick, is a specialist in the architectural history of the great churches and cathedrals of the medieval period.
 She discovered the figure in conducting an intricate survey of  Santiago de Compostela cathedral, a Unesco world heritage site.
 Its significance lies in its association with  the Camino, the pilgrimage across hundreds of miles to reach the shrine of the apostle St James, which is within the cathedral. Such is its draw that. in 2019 alone, the official number of pilgrims reaching the city was some 350,000.
 The current building was begun in the late 11th-century and is a masterpiece of Romanesque architecture, with later additions.
 Alexander was conducting a stone-by-stone analysis to work out its construction sequence, in a project funded by the Galician regional government. It was when she was studying the capitals, about 13 metres above the pavement, that “this little figure popped out”, she recalled.
  “A lovely image of a chap hanging on to the middle of the capital as if his life depended on it. It’s in a row of identical off-the-peg capitals where they’ve been knocking them out in granite – ‘we need another 15 of that design’ – and suddenly there’s one that’s different. So we think it’s the man himself.
 “He emerges out of the capital and is clinging to it. It’s almost as if it’s swallowing him up.”
 The carved figure, which is about 30cm high, is depicted down to his waist. Alexander said: “He’s got a nice little smile. He’s pleased with himself. He’s splendidly carved, with a strongly characterised face.”
 Medieval stonemasons learned their craft through apprenticeships that trained them in the back-breaking trade of cutting stone or using templates to create complex mouldings around doorways and other openings. The most promising masons would learn geometry so that they could design plans and manage construction sites.
 Alexander said: “These masters had to have many skills since they were also responsible for engineering, supply of materials, hiring the workforce and dealing with the patron, who might be a senior member of the clergy or the nobility.”
 She added that such craftsmen have remained anonymous throughout the centuries even into more recent times: “When they were building Liverpool cathedral in the 20th century, they published lists of the craftsmen that worked on the building and they never mentioned stonemasons. So these are the unsung geniuses.”
title: 石刻自拍：900年前在西班牙发现的梅森隐藏的肖像画
title_en: A selfie set in stone: hidden portrait by mason found in Spain 900 years on

你拥有的那台老式雅达利8位电脑(如雅达利800、XL或XE系列)不仅仅是用于复古游戏。如果你把它与新的富士网络适配器和天气程序配对，你就可以获得现场的当地天气信息。这就是它的工作原理！
对于雅达利的爱好者来说，该公司的8位计算机平台还远未消亡。雅达利家用电脑系列于1979年首次推出，曾与Apple II和Commodore 64竞争。它在技术上的灵活性也激发了一直坚持到今天的铁杆粉丝基础。
爱好者经常为这个老式平台发布新软件，然而，很少能找到真正有用的非游戏应用程序。也许这就是为什么当我在推特上发布这个新的雅达利支持的互联网天气程序的照片时，它给很多人留下了深刻的印象。
多亏了FujiNet适配器，我的Atari 800XL现在可以显示从互联网pic.twitter.com/lFTiMB2aVy上拉出的当前天气和7天天气预报。
每个人都想感觉自己有用，人们也喜欢让旧技术变得有用。在这种情况下，这要归功于Atari OpenWeather客户端(或者“Weather.xex”，参考Atari二进制文件)。
该程序是波兰程序员Wojciech Bociański(也称为“bocianu”)的创意。自1985年以来，他一直在使用雅达利电脑，并经常在业余时间为他们编写自己的游戏程序。
Bociański说，他最初创建这个程序是为了测试雅达利的网络能力。因为它变得(出乎意料的)受欢迎，他计划继续更新它，为雅达利的粉丝提供新的功能。
Bocia api ski的程序从openweather(一项通过ń提供数据的服务)获取有关您所在位置的实时天气数据。仅这一点魔力就需要大量的幕后网络数据转换，这已经足够令人印象深刻了。
然而，Weather.xex最引人注目的是它漂亮的显示。它足够漂亮，可以让你的老式监视器或电视整天都在运行，以获得复古气象台的感觉。
Weather.xex不能单独完成它的工作。如上所述，它还需要一个名为富士网的网络适配器。2019年，雅达利自制软件的老手乔·霍诺德(Joe Honold)和托马斯·切里霍姆斯(Thomas Cherryhome)开始寻找一种紧凑的方式，在雅达利电脑上模拟拨号调制解调器，以完成拨打BBS等任务。
他们决定创建自己的Wi-Fi供电设备。其他贡献者也加入进来，机组人员增加了磁盘驱动器仿真、打印功能和模拟其他Atari外围设备的能力。
这款网络适配器连接到雅达利8位计算机上的串行外设(SIO)端口，它正迅速成为平台的瑞士军刀。*FujiNet可与至少有18K RAM的雅达利8位计算机系列的每一个型号配合使用，包括800、1200XL、800XL、XE游戏系统、65XE和130XE。如果没有内存升级，400和600XL将无法工作。
富士网的神奇之处在于其内置的ESP32微控制器，这是一个功能强大的嵌入式计算机模块，具有内置Wi-Fi和蓝牙功能。
有了富士网，你可以直接从互联网上的服务器上拉出游戏，然后在你的雅达利上运行它们。您还可以从microSD卡加载磁盘图像，模拟打印机或盒式磁带驱动器等。
开发人员继续发现这项技术令人惊讶的新应用，未来可能会对硬件进行修订。
当然，要设置富士网络驱动的雅达利气象终端，您需要一台雅达利8位计算机。Weather.xex可以在任何内存为48K及以上的机型上运行(这不包括雅达利400和600XL，除非它们已经升级)。
如果你是白手起家，雅达利800XL是一个很好的模式。它是可靠的，有64K的RAM，在易趣上很容易找到。另一个不错的选择是雅达利130XE，它包括128K的RAM，可以运行更复杂的自制软件。
接下来，您需要一个FujiNet适配器。它是开源的硬件，所以你可以自己构建一个。如果你愿意，你可以从几个供应商那里购买一台，包括酿造学院，老式电脑中心，或者出售我的复古。
如果您有富士网络适配器，请将其插入Atari的SIO端口，然后打开您的Atari。
在FujiNet主程序自动加载后，系统会要求您提供Wi-Fi信息。FujiNet连接到您的网络后，您将在顶部看到一个普通的网络文件系统(TNFS)服务器列表；选择“fujinet.pl”。
连接到fujinet.pl之后，导航到“Networking”目录并选择“weather.xex”。将其分配到FujiNet的驱动器1插槽为只读，按Option重新启动Atari，然后将加载weather.xex。
或者，您可以下载weather.xex二进制文件。将其复制到microSD卡，将其插入您的FujiNet适配器，然后从那里加载。
加载Weather.xex时，程序将尝试根据您的IP地址自动确定您的位置。您可以在启动时按Select并键入您的位置来手动覆盖此设置。
然后你会看到当前当地天气的概况。它每小时和午夜更新一次，以获取新的时间和日期。根据bociański的说法，在该程序的未来版本中，您将能够调整更新频率。不过，现在它会给程序使用的免费API数据密钥带来太大的压力。
总体而言，Weather.xex是一个令人惊叹的程序。然而，它只触及了富士网可能实现的功能的皮毛。根据Cherryholmes的说法，有计划将富士网的概念引入其他复古电脑，如Apple II和Commodore 64。
如果有足够的时间和努力，老式电脑爱好者很可能会继续发现利用旧机器的新的、令人惊叹的方法。

note_en:
That old  Atari eight-bit computer (like the Atari 800, XL, or XE series) you have is useful for more than just retro gaming. If you pair it with the new  FujiNet network adapter and a weather program, you can get live local weather info on it. Here’s how it works!
     For Atari hobbyists, the company’s eight-bit computer platform is far from dead. First introduced in 1979,  the Atari Home Computer series once competed with the  Apple II and the Commodore 64. Its  technical flexibility has also inspired a die-hard fan base that persists to this day.
  Hobbyists regularly release new software for this vintage platform, however, it’s rare to find a non-gaming application that’s actually useful. Perhaps that’s why this new Atari-powered internet weather program impressed so many when I posted images of it in action on Twitter.
 Thanks to a FujiNet adapter, my Atari 800XL can now display the current weather and 7-day forecast pulled from the Internet  pic.twitter.com/lFTiMB2aVy
  Everyone wants to feel useful, and people love to make old technology useful, too. In this case, it’s possible thanks to the Atari OpenWeather client (or “Weather.xex,” in reference to the Atari binary file).
 The program is the brainchild of Polish programmer, Wojciech Bociański (also known as “bocianu”). He’s been using Atari computers since 1985 and often  programs his own games for them in his spare time.
 Bociański said he originally created the program to test the Atari’s network capabilities. Because it’s become (unexpectedly) popular, he plans to continue updating it with new features for Atari fans.
  Bociański’s program pulls live weather data about your location from  OpenWeather, a service that provides the data through an API. That bit of magic alone requires lots of behind-the-scenes network data translation, which is impressive enough.
 However, the most striking thing about Weather.xex is its beautiful display. It’s handsome enough to leave running all day on your vintage monitor or TV to get that retro-weather-station feel.
  Weather.xex doesn’t do its job alone. As mentioned above, it also requires a network adapter called  FujiNet. In 2019, Atari homebrew veterans,  Joe Honold (aka “mozzwald”) and  Thomas Cherryhomes, started looking for a compact way to simulate a dial-up modem on an Atari computer for tasks like  calling a BBS.
 They decided to create their own Wi-Fi-powered device. Other contributors came aboard and the crew added disk drive emulation, printing functionality, and the ability to simulate other Atari peripherals.
    This network adapter connects to the serial peripheral (SIO) port on an Atari eight-bit computer, and it’s quickly becoming the Swiss Army knife of the platform. FujiNet works with every model of the Atari eight-bit computer series that has at least 18K of RAM, including the 800, 1200XL, 800XL, XE Game System, 65XE, and 130XE. The 400 and 600XL won’t work without RAM upgrades.
 The magic of FujiNet is in its internal ESP32 microcontroller, which is a powerful embedded computer module with built-in Wi-Fi and Bluetooth capabilities.
 With FujiNet, you can pull games directly off servers on the internet and run them on your Atari. You can also load disk images from a microSD card, simulate a printer or a cassette drive, and much more.
 Developers continue to discover surprising new applications for the technology and revisions of the hardware are likely in the future.
  To set up a FujiNet-powered Atari weather terminal, you, of course, need an Atari eight-bit computer. Weather.xex will run on any model with 48 K RAM and up (which excludes the Atari 400 and 600XL unless they’ve been upgraded).
 If you’re starting from scratch, the  Atari 800XL is a good model to get. It’s reliable, has 64 K of RAM, and is relatively easy to  find on eBay. Another good option is the  Atari 130XE, which includes 128 K of RAM and can run more sophisticated homebrew software.
  Next, you’ll need a FujiNet adapter. It’s  open-source hardware, so you can build one yourself. If you prefer, you can buy one from several vendors, including  The Brewing Academy,  The Vintage Computer Center, or  Sell My Retro.
 When you have your FujiNet adapter, plug it in to the Atari’s SIO port, and then turn on your Atari.
 After the main FujiNet program loads automatically, you’ll be asked for your Wi-Fi information. After the FujiNet is connected to your network, you’ll see a list of Trivial Network File System (TNFS) servers at the top; select “fujinet.pl.”
  After you’re connected to fujinet.pl, navigate to the “Networking” directory and choose “weather.xex.” Assign it to the Drive 1 slot of your FujiNet as read-only, press Option to reboot your Atari, and then weather.xex will load.
 Alternatively, you can  download the weather.xex binary. Copy it to a microSD card, insert it into your FujiNet adapter, and then load it from there.
  When Weather.xex loads, the program will attempt to automatically determine your location based on your IP address. You can override this manually at startup by pressing Select and typing your location.
 You’ll then see an overview of the current local weather. It updates every hour and at midnight, to fetch the new time and date. According to Bociański, you’ll be able to adjust the update frequency in future versions of the program. Right now, though, it would put too much strain on the free API data key the program uses.
     Overall, Weather.xex is an amazing program. However, it only scratches the surface of what’s possible with FujiNet. According to Cherryholmes, there are plans to bring the FujiNet concept to other retro computers, like the Apple II and Commodore 64.
 Given enough time and effort, it’s likely vintage computer hobbyists will continue to discover new and amazing ways to make use of old machines.
 Benj Edwards  Benj Edwards is a Staff Writer for How-To Geek. For over 14 years, he has written about technology and tech history for sites such as The Atlantic, Fast Company, PCMag, PCWorld, Macworld, Ars Technica, and Wired. In 2005, he created Vintage Computing and Gaming, a blog devoted to tech history. He also created The Culture of Tech podcast and regularly contributes to the Retronauts retrogaming podcast.
